title: Cans, bottles, and glass: rinse, sort, and put out right
excerpt: Drink containers are some of the easiest things to recycle here, as long as you rinse and sort them the way your ward expects.

title: Throwing away a microwave, kettle, or other small appliance
excerpt: Smaller appliances follow easier rules than the four big ones. There are two good routes, depending on size and what is nearby.

title: Cardboard, newspaper, and paper: how to bundle and sort
excerpt: Paper recycling here rewards a little tidiness. Flatten, tie, and keep the piles apart, and none of it gets left behind.

title: Batteries, lighters, and spray cans: the dangerous trash rules
excerpt: A few everyday items can start fires in a garbage truck, so Japan keeps them out of the normal bags. Here is each one.

title: Your first week in Japan: setting up your trash schedule
excerpt: Sorting out the trash in your first week saves a month of confusion. Here is the short version for new arrivals.

title: How to throw away a bicycle in Japan
excerpt: A bike is oversized garbage, but there is a registration step to clear first. Here is the order to do it in.

title: Burnable or non-burnable? The items that trip everyone up
excerpt: The names sound obvious until you are holding something that could go either way. Here are the ones people get wrong.

title: How sodai gomi pickup actually works
excerpt: Oversized garbage has its own booked pickup, separate from normal trash days. Here is the whole loop, start to finish.

title: How to throw away a futon in Japan
excerpt: A futon is too big for the regular bag, so it counts as oversized garbage. Here is the call you have to make and what it costs.

title: Plastic, pura, or PET: which bin does it go in?
excerpt: Three marks show up on almost everything you buy in Japan. Knowing which is which is half the battle with recycling day.

title: Moving out? What to do with your fridge, AC, TV, and washer
excerpt: Four appliances cannot go in the trash at all. Japan has a recycling law for them, and there is a right way to hand them off.
